programs.name: Utah Medicaid Program
programs.tagline: Covers nursing home care, home-based services, and medical costs for Utah seniors on limited incomes.

localEntities.name: Utah County/Wasatch/Summit Counties

programs.intro:
If your loved one is 65+ with limited income and needs help with daily activities like bathing or dressing, Utah Medicaid can cover nursing home care, home-based services, or assisted living. The program has multiple pathways depending on your loved one's care needs and income level — from nursing home coverage where most income goes toward care costs (keeping just $45/month for personal needs) to home waiver programs with income limits of $1,255-$2,901/month.

Asset limits are $2,000 for single applicants or $3,000-$4,000 for couples, but your loved one can keep their home (if equity is under $730,000), one vehicle, and personal belongings. Even if your loved one's income is too high, they may still qualify through the Medically Needy program by "spending down" excess income on medical expenses.

Coverage can start up to 3 months before approval, and the program covers services as long as your loved one remains eligible — potentially for life. Because there are multiple program types with different rules, understanding which pathway fits your loved one's situation is crucial.

summary: Clinical need for daily care assistance

exemptAssets: Primary home (equity ≤$730,000 with intent to return)
Burial plots and irrevocable burial trusts ≤$7,000

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must meet Nursing Home Level of Care (clinical assessment showing need for daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, or medication management) or have functional need for assistance with daily living activities
otherRequirements: Utah resident
U.S. citizen or qualified immigrant
Blind, disabled, or nursing home level of care for long-term care programs

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online through MyCase at medicaid.utah.gov/apply or call 1-866-435-7414 — coverage can begin up to 3 months before approval.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Collect income statements, bank records, medical documentation, and proof of residency before starting your application.

steps.title: Choose application method
steps.description: Apply online through MyCase at medicaid.utah.gov/apply, call the statewide line at 1-866-435-7414, or visit a local office (Utah/Wasatch/Summit Counties: 801-526-9675).

steps.title: Submit complete application
steps.description: Include all required documentation to avoid delays. Ask about the usual processing timeframe when submitting.

steps.title: Complete clinical assessment
steps.description: For long-term care programs, your loved one will need a Nursing Home Level of Care assessment to determine functional eligibility.

steps.title: Follow up on processing
steps.description: Applications can take longer than expected — contact your caseworker if you don't hear back within the estimated timeframe.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Coverage may begin 3 months prior to approval; applications sometimes take longer than expected
applicationGuide.tip: Submit all documents at once to avoid processing delays, and ask specifically about the usual timeframe when applying.

documentsNeeded: Social Security cards for your loved one and spouse
Medicare card (both parts)
Birth certificate or passport for proof of age
Proof of Utah residency (utility bill, lease agreement, or state-issued document)
Most recent Social Security award letter
Pension or retirement income statements
Bank statements for all accounts (last 3 months)
Investment documents (stocks, bonds, annuities, trust agreements)
Property documents (deeds, tax statements)
Vehicle titles and registration
Life insurance policies with face values
Pre-need burial contracts or irrevocable burial trusts
Medical records documenting need for daily care assistance
Proof of citizenship or qualified immigrant status
Documentation of medical expenses paid out-of-pocket (last 3 months) if applying for Medically Needy

contacts.label: Utah Medicaid Statewide Line
contacts.description: Main application and eligibility questions

contacts.label: Utah/Wasatch/Summit Counties
contacts.description: Regional office for northern Utah counties

contacts.label: Regional Office Line
contacts.description: Additional regional contact for application assistance

applicationNotes: Multiple program types exist with different income limits — nursing home Medicaid has no strict income limit but most income pays toward care, while home waiver programs have specific monthly caps
Your loved one's home is exempt from asset limits but subject to estate recovery after death — the state may seek repayment from the home's value
If your loved one's income exceeds the limits, they may still qualify through Medically Needy by spending down excess income on medical expenses
Spousal protections allow a community spouse to keep significant assets ($31,584-$157,920) and income when their partner needs nursing home care
relatedPrograms: Utah Aging and Disability Services Waivers

contentSections.title: Utah Medicaid Program Types
contentSections.description: Utah Medicaid offers different pathways based on your loved one's care needs and income level:

tiers.name: Nursing Home Medicaid
tiers.income: No strict limit — most income pays toward care
tiers.description: Covers full nursing home care costs. Your loved one keeps only $45/month for personal needs.

tiers.name: New Choices Waiver
tiers.description: Home and community-based services to help your loved one stay at home longer.

tiers.name: Aging Waiver
tiers.description: Basic home-based services for lower-income seniors.

tiers.name: Regular ABD Medicaid
tiers.description: Health services for aged, blind, or disabled individuals.

tiers.name: Medically Needy
tiers.description: For those over income limits who spend down through high medical expenses.

faqs.question: Can my loved one keep their house if it's worth more than the $2,000 asset limit?
faqs.answer: Yes, the primary home is exempt from asset limits as long as your loved one intends to return and the equity doesn't exceed $730,000. However, the state may seek repayment from the home's value after your loved one's death through estate recovery.

faqs.question: What if my loved one's income is too high for the waiver programs but they need home care?
faqs.answer: Your loved one may qualify through the Medically Needy program by spending down excess income on medical expenses. The income limit for this pathway is $1,330/month for a single person or $1,803/month for couples (2026 figures).

faqs.question: How does nursing home Medicaid work if my loved one has $3,000/month in Social Security?
faqs.answer: Your loved one can still qualify for nursing home Medicaid regardless of income level. They would pay $2,955/month toward their care costs and keep $45/month for personal needs. Medicaid covers the remaining nursing home expenses.

faqs.question: Can I apply for Utah Medicaid on behalf of my elderly loved one?
faqs.answer: Yes, family members can apply on behalf of elderly loved ones. You'll need legal documentation if you're acting as their representative, and you'll need access to all their financial and medical information for the application.

faqs.question: What happens to my loved one's spouse if one needs nursing home care?
faqs.answer: The community spouse (staying at home) gets special protections. They can keep between $31,584 and $157,920 in assets, plus their own income. These spousal impoverishment protections prevent the at-home spouse from becoming destitute.

faqs.question: Can my loved one apply for both Medicare and Utah Medicaid at the same time?
faqs.answer: Yes, and it's often beneficial to have both. Medicare covers medical services while Medicaid can cover long-term care costs and help pay Medicare premiums. Many Utah Medicaid recipients also have Medicare coverage.

faqs.question: How long does the clinical assessment take for nursing home level of care?
faqs.answer: The Nursing Home Level of Care assessment evaluates whether your loved one needs daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, or medication management. This assessment is separate from the financial eligibility review and must be completed for long-term care programs.

faqs.question: What happens if my loved one's needs change after they're enrolled?
faqs.answer: Your loved one will have periodic reassessments to determine continued eligibility and appropriate level of care. If their needs increase, they may qualify for a different program tier with more comprehensive services.

programs.name: Utah PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly)
programs.tagline: All-in-one medical and long-term care under one team — but not currently available in Utah.
programs.intro:
If your loved one needs nursing home level care but wants to stay home, PACE would provide all their medical care, medications, therapy, and daily support under one coordinated team with no copays or deductibles. Because everything is managed by a single organization, your loved one wouldn't need to coordinate between different doctors, insurance plans, or service providers.

PACE is available in 31 states and serves adults 55+ who qualify for nursing home care but can live safely at home with support. Unfortunately, Utah is one of 19 states that does not offer PACE — there are no providers or service areas anywhere in the state.
programs.savingsSource: Comprehensive medical and social services with no copays or deductibles

summary: Needs nursing home level of care
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must be certified as requiring nursing home level of care (daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, medication management, or mobility)
otherRequirements: Live in a PACE service area (none exist in Utah)
Able to live safely in the community with services
Cannot be enrolled in Medicare Advantage, Medicare prescription drug plan, or hospice

applicationGuide.summary: You cannot apply because Utah does not offer PACE services.

steps.title: Explore Utah alternatives
steps.description: Contact Utah Aging and Adult Services at (801) 538-3910 to learn about Home and Community-Based Services waivers that provide similar in-home support.

steps.title: Consider relocation
steps.description: If PACE is essential for your loved one's care, consider moving to a neighboring state like Colorado or Nevada that offers PACE programs.

applicationGuide.tip: Utah's Home and Community-Based Services waivers provide similar coordination of medical and long-term care services for people who want to avoid nursing home placement.

contacts.label: Utah Aging and Adult Services
contacts.description: State aging services and waiver programs

contacts.label: Utah 2-1-1
contacts.description: Information and referral for all Utah social services

applicationNotes: PACE is fundamentally different from traditional Medicare — it replaces all your loved one's doctors and specialists with one integrated care team
Some families relocate to access PACE if their loved one has complex medical needs that require intensive coordination
relatedPrograms: Utah Home and Community-Based Services Waivers

faqs.question: Could my loved one get PACE if we moved to a neighboring state?
faqs.answer: Yes, Colorado, Nevada, and New Mexico all have PACE programs. Your loved one would need to establish residency and live within the service area of a PACE organization. Each state has different PACE providers with specific geographic boundaries.

faqs.question: What Utah programs provide similar coordinated care?
faqs.answer: Utah's Home and Community-Based Services waivers coordinate medical and personal care services to help people avoid nursing home placement. Contact Utah Aging and Adult Services at (801) 538-3910 to learn about eligibility and waitlists.

faqs.question: Why doesn't Utah offer PACE?
faqs.answer: States must apply to CMS to become PACE states and meet specific requirements for oversight and quality assurance. Utah has not established the regulatory framework needed to approve PACE organizations within the state.

faqs.question: Can I petition Utah to start a PACE program?
faqs.answer: Contact your state legislators or the Utah Department of Health and Human Services to express interest in PACE. State advocacy can influence whether Utah applies to become a PACE state, though the process typically takes several years.

programs.name: Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI)
programs.tagline: If your loved one's monthly income is under $1,823, Medicare may cover their premiums, deductibles, and copays.

localEntities.name: Local Utah Medicaid offices (find via https://medicaid.utah.gov/)

programs.intro:
If your loved one struggles with Medicare premiums and medical bills, three programs can help based on their income level. The Qualified Medicare Beneficiary (QMB) program covers all Medicare costs for individuals earning $1,350/month or less. The Specified Low-income Medicare Beneficiary (SLMB) program covers Part B premiums for those earning $1,351–$1,620/month. The Qualifying Individual (QI) program also covers Part B premiums for those earning $1,621–$1,823/month.

Because these programs work through Utah Medicaid, your loved one gets immediate relief from Medicare costs once approved. QMB recipients receive a Medicaid card marked "MEDICARE COST-SHARING ONLY" that prevents providers from billing them for covered services. SLMB and QI recipients don't get a card because Utah pays Medicare directly for their premiums.

The QI program has limited federal funding that can run out mid-year, blocking new applicants until the following year. QMB and SLMB are guaranteed for all who qualify.
programs.savingsSource: Based on Medicare Part B premium ($185/month) plus typical deductibles and coinsurance

countedAssets: Stocks and bonds over $1,500 face value

otherRequirements: Must be eligible for Medicare Part A (QMB) or enrolled in Part A and Part B (SLMB/QI)
Cannot receive full Medicaid benefits (QI only)
Must be Utah resident
structuredEligibility.povertyLevelReference: QMB: 100% FPL, SLMB: 120% FPL, QI: 135% FPL

applicationGuide.summary: Apply through Utah Medicaid by phone, mail, or in-person with processing taking up to 45 days.

steps.title: Contact Utah Medicaid
steps.description: Visit https://medicaid.utah.gov/medicare-cost-sharing-programs/ or call your local Medicaid office to request an application. No specific form number is required.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Collect proof of income, assets, Medicare enrollment, and Utah residency before submitting your application.

steps.title: Submit complete application
steps.description: Mail, deliver in-person, or submit by phone to your local Utah Medicaid office. Include all supporting documents to avoid delays.

steps.title: Wait for determination
steps.description: QMB decisions take up to 45 days. SLMB and QI can be processed with up to 3 months retroactive coverage if approved.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Up to 45 days for QMB; SLMB and QI may receive up to 3 months retroactive coverage
applicationGuide.waitlist: QI program may stop accepting new applicants when federal funds are exhausted
applicationGuide.tip: Apply early in the year for QI since funding can run out before December

documentsNeeded: Medicare card (both Part A and Part B)
Social Security award letter or most recent statement
Pay stubs from last 30 days (if working)
Bank statements for all accounts (last 3 months)
Investment account statements (stocks, bonds, annuities)
Life insurance policies with cash values
Vehicle titles and registration
Property deeds or tax statements
Proof of Utah residency (utility bill, lease, or state ID)
Birth certificate or proof of age
Burial contracts or funeral expense documentation
Pension or retirement income statements
Any trust agreements or legal documents

contacts.label: Utah Medicaid Information
contacts.description: Find your local Medicaid office and phone number

contacts.label: Utah 2-1-1
contacts.description: Free helpline for all Utah social services and benefits

applicationNotes: Income limits update every April based on federal poverty guidelines — verify current limits when applying
QMB recipients must show their Medicaid card to prevent provider billing for Medicare costs
SLMB and QI recipients won't receive a card because Utah pays Medicare directly for premiums
QI funding is not guaranteed — apply as early in the year as possible to avoid being shut out

contentSections.title: Which Program Level Fits Your Parent?

data.covers: All Medicare premiums, deductibles, coinsurance, and copays

data.card: No card issued — premium paid directly
data.notes: Must be enrolled in Medicare Part A and Part B

data.card: No card issued — premium paid directly
data.notes: Limited funding — may close to new applicants

faqs.question: Can my loved one apply for QMB and still keep their house?
faqs.answer: Yes. The primary home is completely exempt from asset limits regardless of value. Your loved one can own a home worth any amount and still qualify as long as their other countable assets stay under $9,950 (single) or $14,910 (couple).

faqs.question: What happens if my loved one's income is right at the limit?
faqs.answer: Utah uses a $20 monthly income disregard for all programs, meaning $20 of monthly income doesn't count. For example, if your loved one earns $1,370/month, only $1,350 counts toward the QMB limit of $1,350, so they would qualify.

faqs.question: Can I apply for my loved one who has dementia?
faqs.answer: Yes, family members can apply on behalf of elderly loved ones. You'll need to provide all the same documentation plus proof of your authority to act (power of attorney or guardianship papers). Contact Utah Medicaid to discuss the specific process for representative applications.

faqs.question: How is the QI waitlist different from the other programs?
faqs.answer: QI has a federal funding cap that can run out mid-year, while QMB and SLMB are entitlements with guaranteed funding. When QI funds are exhausted, Utah stops accepting new applications until the next federal fiscal year begins in October.

faqs.question: What if my loved one already has full Medicaid coverage?
faqs.answer: Parents with full Medicaid automatically get QMB-level benefits and don't need to apply separately. However, if your loved one has QI coverage, they cannot also have full Medicaid — these programs are mutually exclusive.

faqs.question: Do these programs cover Medicare Advantage plan costs?
faqs.answer: Yes, but it depends on the program level. QMB covers all Medicare Advantage copays, deductibles, and premiums. SLMB and QI only cover the Medicare Part B premium portion, not the additional plan-specific costs.

faqs.question: What happens if providers try to bill my loved one for Medicare costs?
faqs.answer: For QMB recipients, providers cannot legally bill your loved one for any Medicare-covered services. Show the Medicaid card marked 'MEDICARE COST-SHARING ONLY.' If billing continues, contact Utah Medicaid immediately to resolve the issue.

faqs.question: How often does my loved one need to renew their coverage?
faqs.answer: These programs typically require annual renewal through Utah Medicaid. You'll receive renewal paperwork by mail approximately 60 days before the coverage expires. Income and asset limits are updated each April based on federal poverty guidelines.

programs.name: Utah SNAP (Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program)
programs.tagline: Monthly grocery money for Utah seniors 60+ on fixed incomes — up to $415/month loaded on an EBT card.

localEntities.name: Local Utah Workforce Services offices

programs.intro:
If your loved one is 60+ and struggling with grocery costs in Utah, they may qualify for monthly SNAP benefits loaded onto an EBT card. Because Utah has simplified rules for seniors, your loved one doesn't need to pass the gross income test — only net income after deductions for medical expenses over $35/month and high shelter costs.

Because households with seniors get higher asset limits ($4,500 instead of $3,000), your loved one can keep their home, car, and retirement savings and still qualify. A typical 2-person elderly household might receive $415/month after calculations, though the exact amount depends on net income and allowable deductions.

Utah offers ESAP (Elderly Simplified Application Project) for seniors, which means one interview and approval for 3 years instead of frequent recertifications. Despite these benefits, only about half of eligible seniors currently apply for SNAP in Utah.
programs.savingsSource: Based on monthly EBT benefits calculated from net income and household size

otherRequirements: Must live together and buy/prepare food together as household
U.S. citizenship not required - many non-citizens qualify after 5 years or with disability benefits
Include spouse and children under 22 in household even if they buy food separately

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at mycase.utah.gov or call 1-866-526-3663 — seniors get simplified processing with approval for 3 years.

steps.title: Choose application method
steps.description: Apply online at mycase.utah.gov (fastest) or call Utah SNAP hotline at 1-866-526-3663. You can also visit your local Utah Workforce Services office or mail a paper application.

steps.title: Complete SNAP application
steps.description: Fill out the mySNAP application online or request ESAP (Elderly Simplified Application Project) if you're 60+ for easier processing. Include all household members who buy and prepare food together.

steps.title: Submit required documents
steps.description: Upload or mail proof of income, medical expenses over $35/month, shelter costs, and bank statements. Seniors can deduct medical and high shelter costs to lower net income.

steps.title: Complete phone interview
steps.description: Utah Workforce Services will schedule one phone interview. Because you're 60+, this covers you for 3 years instead of frequent recertifications.

steps.title: Receive EBT card
steps.description: If approved, your EBT card arrives by mail within 7-10 days. Benefits are loaded monthly and can be used at grocery stores, farmers markets, and approved retailers.

applicationGuide.processingTime: 30 days for standard processing, faster for ESAP elderly applications with 3-year approval
applicationGuide.tip: Seniors can deduct medical expenses over $35/month and high shelter costs from gross income — gather these receipts to potentially qualify with higher income.

documentsNeeded: Valid photo ID for applicant
Social Security cards for all household members
Social Security award letters or benefit statements
Pension or retirement income documentation
Bank statements for all accounts (last 3 months)
Proof of medical expenses over $35/month (receipts, bills, insurance statements)
Rent receipt or mortgage statement
Utility bills (electric, gas, water, phone)
Proof of Utah residency (utility bill, lease, or state document)
Vehicle registration if you own cars
Life insurance policies with cash values
Investment account statements (stocks, bonds, savings accounts)
Documentation of any other income sources

contacts.label: Utah SNAP Hotline
contacts.description: SNAP applications and questions

contacts.label: Utah 2-1-1
contacts.description: Free helpline for all social services and benefit programs

applicationNotes: ESAP (Elderly Simplified Application Project) lets seniors 60+ skip frequent recertifications — approved for 3 years with just one interview
No gross income limit for households with seniors — you only need to pass the net income test after medical and shelter deductions
Include all people who live together and buy/prepare food together, even if some have separate income
Medical expenses over $35/month can be deducted from income — this includes insurance premiums, prescription costs, and out-of-pocket medical bills
relatedPrograms: Utah LIHEAP (Low Income Home Energy Assistance Program)

faqs.question: Can my loved one keep their house and car if they're worth more than $4,500?
faqs.answer: Yes. The primary home and vehicles are exempt from the $4,500 asset limit. Your loved one can own their home outright and keep their car regardless of value. The $4,500 limit only applies to countable assets like bank accounts, stocks, and cash.

faqs.question: What if my loved one's Social Security is higher than the income limit?
faqs.answer: Because your loved one is 60+, Utah doesn't count gross income — only net income after deductions. Medical expenses over $35/month and high shelter costs reduce the counted income. Many seniors with Social Security above the gross limit still qualify after deductions.

faqs.question: Can I apply for SNAP on behalf of my elderly loved one?
faqs.answer: Yes, you can apply as an authorized representative if your loved one cannot apply themselves due to age or disability. You'll need to complete the application and attend the interview on their behalf. Call 1-866-526-3663 to discuss representative arrangements.

faqs.question: Do both spouses need to be 60+ to get the senior benefits?
faqs.answer: No. If either spouse is 60+ or disabled, the entire household gets the senior rules — no gross income test, $4,500 asset limit instead of $3,000, and simplified recertification every 3 years instead of more frequent reviews.

faqs.question: What happens if my loved one's income changes after approval?
faqs.answer: Report changes within 10 days by calling 1-866-526-3663 or logging into mycase.utah.gov. Because seniors get 3-year certifications through ESAP, minor changes won't require a full reapplication — just updated documentation and possibly adjusted benefit amounts.

faqs.question: Can my loved one get SNAP if they're not a U.S. citizen?
faqs.answer: Many non-citizens qualify for SNAP in Utah. Legal residents who have lived in the U.S. for 5+ years, people receiving disability benefits, and certain other categories are eligible. Citizenship is not required — call 1-866-526-3663 to discuss specific immigration status.

faqs.question: How long does the ESAP approval really last?
faqs.answer: ESAP approvals for elderly households last 3 years with no interim reviews unless you report significant changes. This means one interview and one set of paperwork covers your loved one for 3 years, compared to annual or more frequent recertifications for younger households.

faqs.question: What if my loved one lives in assisted living — do they still qualify?
faqs.answer: It depends on the living arrangement. If your loved one buys and prepares their own food, they can qualify. If the facility provides all meals as part of the fee, they typically cannot get SNAP. Each situation is different — call 1-866-526-3663 to discuss the specific living arrangement.

programs.name: Utah LIHEAP (Low-Income Home Energy Assistance Program)
programs.tagline: Get $190-$850 toward your loved one's heating and cooling bills, plus crisis help up to $2,000

localEntities.name: Statewide via jobs.utah.gov/housing/scso/seal/heat.html local HEAT offices
Five County: fivecountyheat.org
MAG (Summit/Wasatch): magutah.gov/heat
Paiute Tribal (separate): pitu.gov/liheap, 435-586-1112

programs.intro:
If your loved one is struggling with utility bills on a fixed income, Utah's HEAT program (Home Energy Assistance Target) can provide $190 to $850 toward annual heating and cooling costs. Crisis assistance goes up to $2,000 for households facing disconnection or out of fuel.

Your loved one qualifies if their household income is at or below 150% of federal poverty level — that's $1,956/month for a single person or $2,644/month for a two-person household in 2026. The program runs October through September each year, but funds are limited and distributed first-come, first-served.

If your loved one is 60 or older, disabled, or lives with a child under 6, they get priority access starting October 1. Everyone else can apply starting November 1. The money goes directly to the utility company, not to your loved one.
programs.savingsSource: Utah Jobs and Family Services heating/cooling assistance amounts, plus crisis assistance up to $2,000

summary: Income at or below 150% federal poverty level
At least one U.S. citizen in household
otherRequirements: Household responsible for paying home energy costs directly or through rent
At least one adult (18+ or emancipated minor)
At least one U.S. citizen or qualified non-citizen
Household includes all people sharing the address and utility bill

applicationGuide.summary: Contact your regional HEAT office by phone or visit in person — applications typically process within several weeks if funds remain available.

steps.title: Find your regional HEAT office
steps.description: Visit jobs.utah.gov/housing/scso/seal/heat.html to find your local provider, or check eligibility at mydoorway.utah.gov/utility-assistance.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: You'll need proof of all income for the past month, recent utility bills, and ID/citizenship documents for everyone in the household.

steps.title: Apply early if you have priority
steps.description: If your loved one is 60+, disabled, or lives with a child under 6, apply starting October 1. General applications start November 1.

steps.title: Submit your application
steps.description: Call or visit your local HEAT office. Applications stop when annual funds are exhausted, typically several months into the program year.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Several weeks, but funds are limited and distributed first-come, first-served
applicationGuide.waitlist: No waitlist — applications stop when annual funds are exhausted
applicationGuide.tip: Priority households (elderly, disabled, families with young children) should apply immediately when their window opens October 1.

documentsNeeded: Valid government-issued photo ID for all household members
Social Security cards for all household members
Proof of citizenship or qualified non-citizen status
Proof of ALL income for the prior month (pay stubs, Social Security letters, SSI, pension statements)
Recent electric bill showing account number and amount due
Recent gas or propane bills
Documentation of eligible medical expenses from prior month
Proof of child support or alimony received (prior month)
Proof of disability status if claiming priority access
Birth certificate for children under 6 if claiming priority access

contacts.label: Five County HEAT
contacts.description: Serving Iron, Washington, Kane, Garfield, and Beaver counties

contacts.label: MAG HEAT Program
contacts.description: Serving Summit and Wasatch counties

contacts.label: Paiute Tribal LIHEAP
contacts.description: Separate tribal program for Paiute tribal members

contacts.label: Utah 2-1-1
contacts.description: Free helpline to find your local HEAT office

applicationNotes: Priority applications for elderly (60+), disabled, or households with children under 6 start October 1 — general public starts November 1
You cannot apply to both state HEAT and tribal LIHEAP programs
Household definition includes all people sharing the address and utility bill, which is different from SNAP
The program runs year-round but operates on October 1 to September 30 program years — funds typically exhaust before September
relatedPrograms: Utah Low Income Housing Tax Credit

faqs.question: Can my loved one apply if they pay utilities through their rent?
faqs.answer: Yes, as long as they're responsible for energy costs either directly or indirectly through rent. The household must be responsible for paying home energy costs in some form.

faqs.question: What if my loved one missed the priority application period?
faqs.answer: They can still apply during the general application period starting November 1, but funds are distributed first-come, first-served. Priority households (elderly, disabled, families with young children) get served first, so general applications face higher risk of funds being exhausted.

faqs.question: How much will my loved one actually receive?
faqs.answer: Heating and cooling assistance ranges from $190 minimum to $850 maximum based on household income, size, and fuel type. Crisis assistance can go up to $2,000 for households facing disconnection or out of fuel. The exact amount is determined when the application is approved.

faqs.question: Can my loved one apply for crisis help and regular assistance?
faqs.answer: Yes, these are separate components of the same program. Crisis assistance is for immediate emergencies like disconnection notices or running out of fuel, while regular heating/cooling assistance is for ongoing seasonal energy costs.

faqs.question: What happens if my loved one lives on a reservation?
faqs.answer: Tribal members may be eligible for the separate Paiute Tribal LIHEAP program at 435-586-1112, but they cannot apply to both state HEAT and tribal programs. They must choose one.

faqs.question: Does the money go to my loved one or the utility company?
faqs.answer: The assistance is paid directly to the utility vendor or energy provider, not to your loved one. This ensures the funds go toward energy costs and helps prevent disconnection.

faqs.question: What counts as household income for the 150% poverty limit?
faqs.answer: All gross income for all household members from the prior month, including wages, Social Security, SSI, pensions, unemployment, child support, and any other income sources. Medical expenses and some other costs may be deducted.

faqs.question: Can I apply on behalf of my elderly loved one?
faqs.answer: Yes, family members can apply on behalf of elderly or disabled relatives. You'll need all the required documentation and should contact your local HEAT office to discuss the process for representative applications.

programs.name: Utah Weatherization Assistance Program
programs.tagline: Free home energy improvements to reduce your loved one's heating and cooling bills long-term

localEntities.name: Bear River AoG (Box Elder, Cache, Rich, Shoshone Tribe): Logan
Utah Community Action (Davis, Morgan, Weber, Salt Lake, Tooele, Goshute): Salt Lake City
Mountainland Association of Governments (Utah/Provo area): Vineyard
Five County AoG (Beaver, Iron, Washington, Garfield, Kane, Paiute): Cedar City

programs.intro:
If your loved one is struggling with high heating or cooling bills on a fixed income, Utah's Weatherization Assistance Program provides free professional energy improvements to make their home more efficient. Your loved one's home gets a computerized energy audit, then qualified contractors install insulation, seal air leaks, and repair or replace furnaces, air conditioners, or water heaters at no cost.

Priority goes to households with adults 60+, people with disabilities, families with children under 6, or those with especially high energy costs. The program covers homeowners and renters (with landlord permission), and there are no asset limits — just income requirements at or below 200% of federal poverty level.

Because weatherization improvements are permanent, your loved one saves on energy bills for years after the work is completed. The program is administered through seven regional agencies across Utah, each serving specific counties.

summary: Income at or below 200% federal poverty level
Own or rent home in Utah
Home not weatherized in past 15 years
At least one adult U.S. citizen or qualified non-citizen
Priority for elderly, disabled, or high energy costs
otherRequirements: At least one adult in household must be U.S. citizen or qualified non-citizen
Home must not have been weatherized or weatherized more than 15 years ago
Renters need notarized landlord permission form
HEAT program recipients automatically qualify

applicationGuide.summary: Contact your county's weatherization agency by phone, email, or mail — applications are placed on a priority waitlist after eligibility review.

steps.title: Find your county's agency
steps.description: Salt Lake/Davis/Weber/Morgan/Tooele counties call Utah Community Action at (801) 214-3215. Cache/Box Elder/Rich counties call Bear River AoG at (435) 752-7242. Utah County area call Mountainland at (801) 229-3800. Southern Utah counties call Five County AoG at (435) 865-0195.

steps.title: Submit application and documents
steps.description: Complete the weatherization application and provide income verification, utility bills, Social Security cards, and property ownership proof. Mail, email, fax, or drop off to your regional agency.

steps.title: Wait for eligibility determination
steps.description: Agency reviews your application and determines eligibility. If approved, you're placed on a priority waitlist based on household composition and energy costs.

steps.title: Schedule energy audit
steps.description: When your turn comes, a contractor performs a computerized energy audit to determine which improvements will save the most energy in your specific home.

steps.title: Complete weatherization work
steps.description: Approved contractors install insulation, seal air leaks, repair or replace heating/cooling equipment, and conduct safety inspections at no cost to you.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Processing time varies by agency and demand
applicationGuide.waitlist: Yes, applications are prioritized by need (elderly, disabled, families with young children) and placed on waiting list
applicationGuide.tip: HEAT program recipients get priority placement — mention if your loved one receives heating assistance when applying.

documentsNeeded: Social Security cards for all household members
Most recent utility bill (electric, gas, or propane)
Bill History Release Authorization form (provided by agency)
Proof of income for last 30 days (pay stubs, Social Security award letter, pension statements)
Property deed or mortgage statement for homeowners
Notarized Weatherization Rental Agreement for renters
HEAT program approval letter if participating
Verification of all earned and unearned income sources
Proof of citizenship or legal residency for required household member

contacts.label: Utah Community Action (Salt Lake, Davis, Weber, Morgan, Tooele counties)
contacts.description: Weatherization applications and information

contacts.label: Bear River AoG (Cache, Box Elder, Rich counties)
contacts.description: Weatherization applications for northern Utah

contacts.label: Mountainland AoG (Utah County area)
contacts.description: Weatherization for central Utah counties

contacts.label: Five County AoG (Southern Utah)
contacts.description: Weatherization for Beaver, Iron, Washington, Garfield, Kane counties

applicationNotes: HEAT program recipients get priority processing — mention this when applying if your loved one receives heating assistance
Renters must get landlord permission before applying — the notarized agreement is required before work can begin
Each agency serves specific counties only — calling the wrong agency will delay your application
Priority waitlists favor elderly, disabled, and households with children under 6 — others may wait significantly longer

faqs.question: Can my loved one apply if they're renting their home?
faqs.answer: Yes, renters are eligible but need a notarized Weatherization Rental Agreement signed by the landlord before any work can begin. The agency provides this form. Many landlords agree because the improvements increase property value at no cost to them.

faqs.question: What if my loved one's home was weatherized years ago?
faqs.answer: Homes are eligible if they've never been weatherized or if weatherization was completed more than 15 years ago. The energy audit will determine eligibility based on the home's current condition and past improvements.

faqs.question: How long is the waitlist really?
faqs.answer: Waitlist times vary significantly by region and priority level. Elderly, disabled, and households with children under 6 get priority placement. Others may wait months to over a year depending on local demand and funding.

faqs.question: Can I apply on behalf of my elderly loved one?
faqs.answer: Yes, family members can help with the application process, but the eligible household member must sign the application. All income and household information must be for your loved one's household, not yours.

faqs.question: What if my loved one receives HEAT assistance?
faqs.answer: HEAT recipients automatically meet income requirements and get priority placement on the waitlist. Bring the HEAT approval letter when applying — this can significantly reduce wait times.

faqs.question: Can my loved one apply for both weatherization and HEAT?
faqs.answer: Yes, these are separate programs that work well together. HEAT provides immediate help with energy bills while weatherization provides long-term energy savings through home improvements. Apply for both if eligible.

faqs.question: What happens if my loved one's income is slightly over the limit?
faqs.answer: Income limits are firm at 200% of federal poverty level ($2,510/month for a single person in 2026). However, if your loved one pays high medical expenses, ask the agency about allowable deductions that might bring gross income under the threshold.

faqs.question: Which agency do I call if my loved one lives on the border between counties?
faqs.answer: Contact the agency for the county where your loved one's home is physically located. Cache County calls Bear River AoG at (435) 752-7242. Salt Lake County calls Utah Community Action at (801) 214-3215. Each agency only serves specific counties.

programs.name: Utah SHIP (State Health Insurance Assistance Program)
programs.tagline: Free one-on-one Medicare counseling to help your loved one navigate coverage options and find cost assistance programs.

localEntities.name: Primary contact: Utah Department of Human Services, 195 North 1950 West, Salt Lake City, UT 84116

programs.intro:
If your loved one is on Medicare or turning 65 soon, Utah SHIP provides free, personalized counseling to help them understand their options. SHIP counselors explain Medicare Parts A, B, C, and D, help compare Medigap policies, and walk through enrollment deadlines and penalties.

Because SHIP counselors are trained specifically on Medicare rules and receive 8-10 hours of intensive program training, they can help identify cost-assistance programs your loved one may qualify for — including Medicare Savings Programs that can cover premiums and Extra Help for prescription drugs. The service is available to all Medicare beneficiaries regardless of income, and family caregivers can also access counseling on behalf of their loved ones.

summary: Family caregivers can also access services
otherRequirements: Must be eligible for Medicare or will be eligible for Medicare soon
Family members and caregivers of Medicare beneficiaries can also access SHIP services

applicationGuide.summary: Call Utah SHIP at 1-800-541-7735 to schedule free counseling — no application needed.

steps.title: Call Utah SHIP
steps.description: Call 1-800-541-7735 (Utah SHIP toll-free number) to schedule counseling. You can also call the national SHIP line at 1-877-839-2675 or email dhotton@utah.gov.

steps.title: Gather your information
steps.description: Ask what information to bring when you schedule. Generally helpful: Medicare card, current insurance policies, prescription drug information, and financial documents if exploring cost-assistance programs.

steps.title: Meet with counselor
steps.description: Work one-on-one with a trained counselor to understand Medicare options, enrollment timing, and identify programs that could reduce costs.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Immediate consultation available. Counselors suggest contacting SHIP 3 months before Medicare starts.
applicationGuide.tip: Contact SHIP 3 months before your loved one's Medicare starts, but assistance is available at any point after that.

contacts.label: Utah SHIP Toll-Free
contacts.description: Utah's State Health Insurance Assistance Program

contacts.label: National SHIP Program
contacts.description: National SHIP helpline for Medicare assistance

contacts.label: Utah Department of Human Services
contacts.description: In-person assistance at 195 North 1950 West, Salt Lake City, UT 84116

faqs.question: Can I get SHIP counseling on behalf of my elderly loved one?
faqs.answer: Yes. Family members and caregivers of Medicare beneficiaries can access SHIP services. When you call 1-800-541-7735, let them know you're calling for your loved one and ask what information you'll need to bring to the counseling session.

faqs.question: Will SHIP help my loved one apply for Medicare cost assistance programs?
faqs.answer: SHIP counselors can help identify if your loved one qualifies for Medicare Savings Programs or Extra Help prescription assistance, but you must apply separately for those programs through the Social Security Administration or other channels. SHIP provides guidance and information, not direct enrollment.

faqs.question: Is this the same as CHIP for children?
faqs.answer: No. Utah SHIP serves Medicare beneficiaries (typically 65+), while CHIP is Utah's Children's Health Insurance Program for children under 19. These are completely different programs with different eligibility and services.

faqs.question: Does SHIP sell insurance or get paid by insurance companies?
faqs.answer: No. SHIP provides free, unbiased counseling and does not sell insurance or receive commissions. The service is federally funded to provide objective Medicare guidance to beneficiaries and their families.

programs.name: Utah Meals on Wheels
programs.tagline: Free home-delivered meals for parents 60+ who can't shop or cook due to mobility challenges

localEntities.name: Davis County Aging Services
Mountainland Assoc. of Govts (MAG) for Utah/Wasatch/Summit Counties
Cache County Senior Center
Local AAAs per region

programs.intro:
If your loved one is 60+ and struggles to leave home to shop for groceries or cook meals, Utah Meals on Wheels delivers hot, nutritious lunches directly to their door Monday through Friday. Each meal provides one-third of daily nutritional needs and includes protein, vegetables, fruit, grains, and milk — with no income limits or asset tests.

Because the program requires your loved one to be homebound (unable to leave home without assistance) and have an in-person assessment, delivery is only available within specific service zones that vary by county. The suggested donation is $3.50-$4 per meal but is completely voluntary — no one is denied meals for inability to pay.

Delivery volunteers also provide a daily wellness check during each visit. Some areas offer weekend meals delivered Thursday or Friday, while high-demand counties like Utah County currently have waitlists.

summary: Unable to shop or cook meals
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must be homebound (unable to leave home without assistance) and unable to shop or prepare meals due to mobility challenges, disability, or frailty. This is assessed annually through an in-person evaluation.
otherRequirements: Must live within local program's delivery zone
Cannot drive a vehicle (in some counties like Davis)
Priority given to isolated individuals

applicationGuide.summary: Call your local Area Agency on Aging for an in-person assessment, which typically happens within a week of contact.

steps.title: Contact your county program
steps.description: Call your local Area Agency on Aging: Davis County (801-525-5099), Utah/Wasatch/Summit Counties (801-229-3802), Cache County (435-755-1720), or find others through the state aging office.

steps.title: Schedule assessment
steps.description: A staff member will schedule an in-person visit to assess your loved one's homebound status, mobility challenges, and dietary needs.

steps.title: Complete assessment
steps.description: The assessor will verify age, residence, and document why your loved one qualifies as homebound and unable to shop or cook.

steps.title: Start delivery
steps.description: If approved and no waitlist, meals begin delivery Monday-Friday. Cancel by 9am if your loved one won't be home.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Some programs start within a week, others have longer waits due to waitlists
applicationGuide.waitlist: Yes, some areas like Utah County have had waitlists since January 2022
applicationGuide.tip: Call early in the day — meals aren't left if your loved one isn't home and cancellations must be made by 9am.

documentsNeeded: Valid government-issued photo ID or birth certificate (proof of age 60+)
Proof of residence within the delivery zone (utility bill, lease agreement, or mail)
Medical information documenting homebound status or mobility limitations
Emergency contact information
List of current medications (for special dietary meal planning)
Information about other household members who may also qualify

contacts.label: Davis County Aging Services
contacts.description: Davis County Meals on Wheels program

contacts.label: MAG Utah Counties
contacts.description: Utah, Wasatch, and Summit counties program

contacts.label: Cache County Senior Center
contacts.description: Cache County meals program

contacts.label: Utah Division of Aging
contacts.description: Statewide aging services information

applicationNotes: Each county operates independently with different providers, costs, and waitlist lengths — call your specific county program
The homebound requirement is strictly enforced and reassessed annually — parents who can easily leave home or have regular caregivers for shopping may not qualify
Weekend meals are available in some areas but must be requested separately and delivered Thursday or Friday
Under-60 spouses or disabled dependents may qualify in some programs, often with payment required

faqs.question: What if my loved one can still drive but has trouble cooking?
faqs.answer: Your loved one must be both homebound (unable to leave without assistance) AND unable to shop or cook. In some counties like Davis, owning or driving a vehicle automatically disqualifies them. Contact your local program to discuss specific mobility limitations versus driving ability.

faqs.question: Can my loved one get weekend meals too?
faqs.answer: Weekend meals are available in some areas, typically delivered Thursday or Friday for Saturday and Sunday. Not all counties offer this service, and it usually requires a separate request during the assessment process.

faqs.question: How long is the waitlist in Utah County?
faqs.answer: Utah County has had a waitlist since January 2022 with no specific timeline given for new applicants. Other counties may have immediate availability. Call your specific county program to check current wait times in your area.

faqs.question: What happens if my loved one isn't home when meals are delivered?
faqs.answer: Meals are not left if your loved one isn't home for safety and food quality reasons. You must cancel by 9am the day of delivery. Repeated missed deliveries without cancellation may affect continued service.

faqs.question: Can I apply on behalf of my elderly loved one?
faqs.answer: Yes, adult children can initiate the contact and application process, but an in-person assessment of your loved one is still required. The assessor needs to directly evaluate your loved one's homebound status and dietary needs.

faqs.question: Does my loved one have to pay the suggested donation?
faqs.answer: No, the $3.50-$4 suggested donation per meal is completely voluntary. No one is denied meals for inability to pay. However, donations help expand the program to serve more people in the community.

programs.name: Utah Caregiver Support Program (UCSP)
programs.tagline: Up to $1,500/year in respite care, personal care aides, and support services for family caregivers.

localEntities.name: Salt Lake County Aging & Adult Services: 801-468-2460
Davis County: 801-451-3377
Weber/Morgan Counties: 801-625-3866
Contact DAAS for other counties: https://daas.utah.gov/seniors/

programs.intro:
If you're caring for an aging loved one who needs help with daily activities like bathing or dressing, Utah's Caregiver Support Program provides up to $1,500 in services each year to help you manage. The program covers respite care, personal care aides, adult day care, overnight respite, case management, caregiver support groups, counseling, and training — services that typically cost $15-25/hour out of pocket.

Because this program has no income limits but requires you to demonstrate caregiver stress through a formal assessment, eligibility depends on your caregiving burden rather than your financial situation. Your loved one must be 60+ (or any age with Alzheimer's or dementia) and need help with at least two daily activities.

The program operates through county Area Agencies on Aging and often maintains a waiting list. Caregivers 55+ and those with the highest stress scores get priority.
programs.savingsSource: Service value based on $15-25/hour typical cost for respite and personal care services

summary: Care recipient 60+ or any age with dementia
Care recipient needs help with 2+ daily activities
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Care recipient must have deficits in at least two activities of daily living (bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, transferring, or walking)
otherRequirements: Must be unpaid family caregiver or informal caregiver
Must demonstrate medium-to-high stress on DAAS assessment tools
Must complete demographic intake and caregiver burden screening

applicationGuide.summary: Call your county Area Agency on Aging for assessment — expect several weeks to months on waiting list.

steps.title: Contact your county Area Agency on Aging
steps.description: Salt Lake County: 801-468-2460, Davis County: 801-451-3377, Weber/Morgan Counties: 801-625-3866. For other counties, visit daas.utah.gov/seniors/ to find your local office.

steps.title: Complete intake screening
steps.description: Complete DAAS Approved Demographic Intake and Screening tool over the phone or in person to determine initial eligibility and priority level.

steps.title: Assessment appointment
steps.description: If you qualify for services, schedule a comprehensive assessment to complete the DAAS-approved Caregiver Burden score and determine specific service needs.

steps.title: Wait for services
steps.description: You'll be placed on a priority list based on your assessment scores. Older caregivers and those with highest stress scores are served first.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Not specified — program often maintains waiting list
applicationGuide.waitlist: Yes, program often maintains a waiting list; applicants served in priority order based on assessment scores
applicationGuide.tip: Priority goes to caregivers 55+ with greatest social and economic need — be thorough when describing your stress level and caregiving challenges during the assessment.

documentsNeeded: Government-issued photo ID for caregiver
Birth certificate or proof of age for care recipient
Medical documentation showing care recipient's functional limitations or diagnosis
Proof of Utah residency (utility bill, lease, or state-issued document)
List of current medications and medical providers for care recipient
Documentation of current caregiving responsibilities and daily care schedule
Emergency contact information for care recipient
Insurance cards (Medicare, Medicaid, private insurance) for care recipient
Power of attorney or guardianship documents if applicable

contacts.label: Salt Lake County Aging & Adult Services
contacts.description: UCSP intake and assessment for Salt Lake County residents

contacts.label: Davis County Area Agency on Aging
contacts.description: UCSP services for Davis County residents

contacts.label: Weber/Morgan Counties
contacts.description: Care for the Caregiver Program serving Weber and Morgan Counties

contacts.label: Utah DAAS
contacts.description: Statewide program information and county office referrals

applicationNotes: No income requirement means eligibility is broader than most programs, but you must demonstrate significant caregiver stress — this isn't automatic
Services are capped at $1,500 per 12-month period — plan carefully if you need ongoing intensive support
Different counties may have different wait times and service availability — ask about current waiting list length when you call
Caregivers 55+ get priority, but younger caregivers can still qualify if they demonstrate high stress levels
relatedPrograms: Utah Senior Farmers Market Nutrition Program
Area Agency on Aging Senior Services

faqs.question: Can I qualify even if my family has a good income?
faqs.answer: Yes. Utah's Caregiver Support Program has no income limits or caps. Eligibility is based entirely on your caregiver stress level and your loved one's functional needs, not your financial situation.

faqs.question: How long is the waiting list really?
faqs.answer: Wait times vary by county and your priority score. Caregivers 55+ with the highest stress scores are served first. Some counties may have several months wait, while others could be longer.

faqs.question: What if my loved one only needs help with one daily activity like bathing?
faqs.answer: Your loved one must have deficits in at least two activities of daily living to qualify. Single-activity limitations typically don't meet the program requirements.

faqs.question: Can I get more than $1,500 if my needs are really high?
faqs.answer: No. The program caps services at $1,500 per 12-month period regardless of need level. If you need more intensive support, ask your case worker about other programs you might qualify for.

faqs.question: What if I'm already getting paid to care for my loved one through another program?
faqs.answer: You cannot qualify for UCSP if you're receiving payment for caregiving through Medicaid waivers or other programs. The caregiver must be unpaid and informal.

faqs.question: Can my loved one have Alzheimer's even if they're under 60?
faqs.answer: Yes. Care recipients of any age with Alzheimer's disease or related neurological disorders qualify, even if they're younger than 60. The age requirement only applies to general caregiving situations.

faqs.question: What happens if we move to a different county in Utah?
faqs.answer: You'll need to re-apply with your new county's Area Agency on Aging. Each county administers the program separately, so your case won't automatically transfer.

faqs.question: Can I apply for this program and Medicaid waiver services at the same time?
faqs.answer: You can apply for both, but if you start receiving paid caregiver services through Medicaid, you'll no longer qualify for UCSP since it requires unpaid caregiving.

programs.name: Senior Community Service Employment Program (SCSEP)
programs.tagline: Part-time paid work training for unemployed adults 55+ earning under $2,500/month for a family of two

localEntities.name: Easterseals-Goodwill Northern Rocky Mountain

programs.intro:
If your loved one is 55+ and unemployed with limited income, SCSEP provides paid part-time work training up to 20 hours per week at community organizations. Your loved one earns wages while gaining job skills in fields like customer service, health care, or computer technology — creating a bridge to permanent employment.

Your loved one's family income must be below 125% of the federal poverty level ($2,500/month for a household of two in 2026). Because this isn't first-come, first-served, the program director reviews each application for suitability and may maintain a waitlist. Veterans and adults over 65 get priority for available positions.

Participants can stay in the program for up to 48 months total across their lifetime. The goal is to develop skills and connections that lead to unsubsidized employment in the community.

otherRequirements: Must be unemployed or have notice of job termination
Legally authorized to work in the U.S.
Resident of Utah in an area served by the program
Have not exceeded 48-month lifetime participation limit
Register with Utah State Workforce within two weeks

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at ipdcscsep.org or call (800) 771-2153 — processing time varies and acceptance isn't guaranteed.

steps.title: Complete the SCSEP application
steps.description: Fill out the application at https://www.ipdcscsep.org/scsep-application or call Easterseals-Goodwill at (800) 771-2153 for a paper application.

steps.title: Gather income verification documents
steps.description: Collect proof of all income sources, age verification, Utah residency proof, and work authorization documents.

steps.title: Register with Utah State Workforce
steps.description: If not already registered, sign up at jobs.utah.gov within two weeks of submitting your SCSEP application.

steps.title: Wait for Project Director review
steps.description: Your application will be reviewed for eligibility and suitability — this is not first-come, first-served. You may be placed on a waitlist.

steps.title: Complete Individual Employment Plan
steps.description: If accepted, work with staff to create your personalized training and employment goals plan.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Not specified — application reviewed by Project Director based on eligibility and suitability
applicationGuide.waitlist: Possible waitlist if no immediate openings after eligibility determination
applicationGuide.tip: Veterans and their qualified spouses get first priority, followed by adults over 65 — mention veteran status when applying.

documentsNeeded: Proof of age (birth certificate, driver's license, or passport)
Social Security card
Proof of all income sources from the last 30 days (pay stubs, Social Security letters, pension statements)
Proof of Utah residency (utility bill, lease agreement, or state-issued document)
Valid government-issued photo ID
Proof of legal work authorization in the U.S. (for I-9 form completion)
Bank account information for direct deposit setup
Proof of Utah State Workforce registration (or register within two weeks)
Termination notice if currently employed but facing job loss
Military service records (DD-214) if claiming veteran priority

contacts.label: Easterseals-Goodwill SCSEP Utah
contacts.description: Primary SCSEP provider for Utah applications and enrollment

contacts.label: Utah 2-1-1
contacts.description: General information about employment and social services

applicationNotes: Enrollment decisions are based on suitability and priority tiers, not application order — veterans and adults over 65 get preference
The 48-month lifetime limit applies even if you switch between different SCSEP providers across states
You must be willing to work up to 20 hours per week and follow your Individual Employment Plan requirements
Willfully misreporting income information will disqualify you from the program

faqs.question: Can my loved one apply if they're currently working part-time but worried about losing their job?
faqs.answer: Yes, if your loved one has received notice of pending termination or layoff, they can apply even while still employed. They need to provide the termination notice as part of their application.

faqs.question: What happens if my loved one has already used SCSEP in another state — does that count toward the 48-month limit?
faqs.answer: Yes, the 48-month limit applies across all SCSEP providers nationwide. If your loved one participated in SCSEP in California for 12 months, they would have 36 months of eligibility remaining for Utah SCSEP.

faqs.question: How long is the waitlist really, and will we know where my loved one stands?
faqs.answer: The program doesn't publish specific waitlist times since acceptance depends on suitability assessment, not application order. Veterans and adults over 65 get priority, which can affect wait times for others.

faqs.question: Can my loved one choose what type of work they'll be trained for?
faqs.answer: Your loved one will work with staff to create an Individual Employment Plan based on their interests and local opportunities. Common placements include customer service, health care support, computer technician roles, and teachers' aide positions.

faqs.question: What if my loved one needs to move to a different area of Utah during the program?
faqs.answer: Since SCSEP isn't available statewide, your loved one would need to move to an area served by Easterseals-Goodwill. Call (800) 771-2153 to confirm whether their new location has program coverage.

faqs.question: Can I apply on behalf of my elderly loved one who has trouble with paperwork?
faqs.answer: You can help your loved one complete the application, but they must be legally able to work and participate in the program themselves. The program requires active participation in work training, not just benefit receipt.

faqs.question: What happens if my loved one's income changes while they're in the program?
faqs.answer: Income is verified at enrollment, but participants must report significant changes. If income rises above 125% of the federal poverty level, your loved one may need to transition out of the program.

faqs.question: Can my loved one apply for SCSEP and regular unemployment benefits at the same time?
faqs.answer: Check with both programs about simultaneous enrollment. SCSEP requires registration with Utah State Workforce, which also handles unemployment benefits, so they can provide guidance on any conflicts.

programs.name: Utah Legal Services for Seniors
programs.tagline: Free legal help for seniors 60+ with public benefits, elder abuse, housing, and estate planning issues.

localEntities.name: Utah Legal Services main site; Salt Lake County Senior Citizen Law Center; Timpanogos Legal Center (Utah, Wasatch, Summit Counties)

programs.intro:
If your loved one is 60+ and needs legal help with Social Security, Medicare, elder abuse, or estate planning, they may qualify for free legal services in Utah. Utah Legal Services provides attorneys and legal advocates at no cost for seniors with limited income.

Because the program covers everything from benefit appeals to powers of attorney, your loved one gets professional legal help without the typical $200-500/hour attorney fees. The program handles senior-specific legal issues including public benefits disputes, guardianship proceedings, housing problems, consumer fraud, and wills.

Your loved one must be 60+ with income at or below 125% of federal poverty guidelines ($18,310/year for a single person in 2024). In cases of documented hardship like high medical expenses, income up to 200% of poverty guidelines ($29,160/year) may be allowed with approval.

otherRequirements: Legal issue must relate to senior-specific problems like public benefits, housing, elder abuse, guardianship, or estate planning
structuredEligibility.povertyLevelReference: 125% FPL (exceptions to 200% FPL with hardship documentation)

applicationGuide.summary: Call Utah Legal Services for intake screening — approval depends on income verification and case type.

steps.title: Call for intake
steps.description: Contact Utah Legal Services at (801) 328-8849 or your regional office. For Utah County, call Timpanogos Legal Center at (801) 649-8895.

steps.title: Complete intake form
steps.description: Provide income, asset, and household information. Some offices email forms; others complete by phone.

steps.title: Submit documentation
steps.description: Provide proof of income, assets, and details about your legal issue for screening.

steps.title: Wait for approval
steps.description: Staff reviews your case and income eligibility. If approved, you'll be matched with an attorney or legal advocate.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Not specified — varies by case complexity and attorney availability
applicationGuide.tip: Document any hardship expenses (medical bills, disability costs) if your income is above 125% FPL — you may still qualify up to 200% with Executive Director approval.

documentsNeeded: Proof of age (driver's license, birth certificate, or state ID)
Social Security award letters for all household members
Bank statements for all accounts (last 3 months)
Documentation of any hardship expenses (medical bills, disability-related costs)
Details about your legal issue (benefit denial letters, court papers, housing notices)
Proof of household size and composition

contacts.label: Utah Legal Services Main Line
contacts.description: Statewide intake and general information

contacts.label: Timpanogos Legal Center
contacts.description: Serves Utah, Wasatch, and Summit counties

contacts.label: Utah 2-1-1
contacts.description: 24/7 helpline for all social services and referrals

applicationNotes: Income exceptions up to 200% FPL require Executive Director approval and documented hardship — don't assume automatic approval
Some services are provided through county contracts or volunteer attorneys, so availability may vary by region

faqs.question: Can I apply on behalf of my elderly loved one who has dementia?
faqs.answer: Yes, if you have legal authority through power of attorney or guardianship. If your loved one cannot make legal decisions and you don't have legal authority, Utah Legal Services can help with guardianship proceedings. Bring documentation of your loved one's condition and any existing legal documents.

faqs.question: What if my loved one's income is slightly above the limit but they have high medical expenses?
faqs.answer: Your loved one may still qualify up to 200% of federal poverty guidelines ($29,160/year for a single person) if they can document economic hardship like high medical expenses or disability-related costs. This requires Executive Director approval, so gather all medical bills and expense documentation before applying.

faqs.question: Does this cover help with Social Security disability appeals?
faqs.answer: Yes, Utah Legal Services handles public benefits cases including Social Security, SSI, and disability appeals. Because these cases have strict deadlines, call immediately if your loved one received a denial letter — you typically have only 60 days to appeal.

faqs.question: Can my loved one get help with a will even if they don't qualify for other services?
faqs.answer: Yes, wills and estate planning are handled through the Senior Law Project using volunteer attorneys, which may have different eligibility requirements than other services. Call Utah Legal Services to ask specifically about will preparation for seniors.

programs.name: Utah Long-Term Care Ombudsman Program
programs.tagline: Free advocacy for your loved one if they're already in a nursing home, assisted living, or memory care facility.

localEntities.name: State-level program manager
11 local Area Agencies on Aging programs
Salt Lake County Aging and Adult Services
Mountain Association of Governments (Summit, Utah, Wasatch counties)

programs.intro:
If your loved one lives in a nursing home, assisted living, memory care, or skilled rehab facility and faces problems with care, billing, or their rights, the Utah Long-Term Care Ombudsman Program provides free advocacy. Approximately 50 trained staff and volunteer ombudsmen across 11 regional offices investigate complaints, resolve disputes, and ensure facilities respect residents' rights.

This program serves any resident already living in a long-term care facility — no age, income, or asset requirements. Because ombudsmen operate independently from facility regulators, they focus purely on advocacy and problem-solving rather than enforcement. Services are completely confidential except in life-threatening emergencies.

Important: This program advocates for people already in facilities. It does not help with funding care, finding facilities, or getting admitted to care.

summary: Already living in a long-term care facility
otherRequirements: Must be a current resident of a nursing home, assisted living, skilled rehab, memory care, or small health care facility
Services also available to individuals attempting to enter a facility

applicationGuide.summary: Call your local Area Agency on Aging ombudsman program — no forms or documentation required.

steps.title: Contact your local ombudsman
steps.description: Call your regional Area Agency on Aging to reach the ombudsman serving your loved one's facility. For example, Mountain Association of Governments serves Summit, Utah, and Wasatch counties at 801-229-3809.

steps.title: Describe the situation
steps.description: Explain the problem your loved one is experiencing — billing issues, care quality, facility policies, or rights violations. The ombudsman will determine next steps.

steps.title: Ombudsman investigates
steps.description: The ombudsman visits the facility, interviews residents and staff, and works to resolve the issue through advocacy and mediation.

applicationGuide.tip: You can file a complaint on behalf of your loved one, but the ombudsman will need your loved one's permission to investigate unless it's an emergency.

urls.label: Utah Division of Aging and Adult Services

contacts.label: Mountain Association of Governments
contacts.description: Serves Summit, Utah, and Wasatch counties

contacts.label: Utah Aging and Adult Services
contacts.description: State program information and county contact referrals

faqs.question: Can I file a complaint if my loved one has dementia and can't speak for themselves?
faqs.answer: Yes, family members can file complaints on behalf of residents. The ombudsman may still need to speak with your loved one if possible, but they understand cognitive limitations and will work within those constraints. In cases involving immediate safety concerns, the ombudsman can act without explicit resident consent.

faqs.question: What's the difference between calling the ombudsman versus reporting to state regulators?
faqs.answer: Ombudsmen focus on advocacy and problem-solving — they work with facilities to resolve issues and protect residents' rights. State regulators investigate compliance violations and can impose penalties. The ombudsman often resolves problems faster through direct facility engagement, while regulatory complaints may take longer but can result in formal citations.

programs.name: Utah Aging Waiver
programs.tagline: Helps your loved one get personal care, home modifications, and other services to stay home instead of moving to a nursing facility.

localEntities.name: Local Aging & Adult Services offices by county, in partnership with Utah Medicaid

programs.intro:
If your loved one is 65+ and needs daily help with bathing, dressing, or managing medications, Utah Aging Waiver can provide personal care, home modifications, respite care, and other services to help them stay home instead of moving to a nursing facility. The program covers case management, adult day care, specialized medical equipment, non-medical transportation, and personal emergency response systems — all tailored to your loved one's specific needs through a care plan.

To qualify, your loved one must be 65+ with income below $1,305/month (effective March 2025–February 2026) and countable assets under $2,000. They must also meet Nursing Facility Level of Care (a clinical assessment of whether your loved one needs significant physical assistance with at least 2 daily activities like bathing or dressing, has poor orientation requiring nursing facility care, or has a medical condition that cannot be safely managed without waiver services). The program is available statewide through your county's Aging & Adult Services office.

summary: Need daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication management

exemptAssets: Burial plots and irrevocable burial trusts up to $7,000 each

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must meet Nursing Facility Level of Care (requires 2 of 3 conditions: significant physical assistance with at least 2 daily activities like toileting, bathing, dressing, transferring, mobility, or eating; poor orientation requiring nursing facility care; or medical condition that cannot be safely managed without waiver services)
otherRequirements: Utah resident
U.S. citizen or qualified immigrant

applicationGuide.summary: Call your county's Aging & Adult Services office for phone intake and assessment — processing time varies.

steps.title: Contact your county office
steps.description: Call your local Aging & Adult Services office for phone intake. They will connect you with a registered nurse or case manager to begin the assessment process.

steps.title: Complete financial screening
steps.description: Provide income and asset documentation to determine if your loved one meets the financial eligibility requirements ($1,305/month income limit and $2,000 asset limit).

steps.title: Undergo clinical assessment
steps.description: A registered nurse will use the InterRAI MDS-HC assessment tool to determine if your loved one meets Nursing Facility Level of Care based on daily living assistance needs and medical condition.

steps.title: Develop care plan
steps.description: If approved, work with a case manager to create a personalized plan of care that identifies which services your loved one needs (personal care, home modifications, respite, etc.).

applicationGuide.tip: Dementia diagnosis alone is not sufficient to meet the clinical requirements — your loved one must need significant physical assistance with daily activities or have poor orientation requiring nursing facility-level care.

documentsNeeded: Social Security card
Birth certificate or proof of age
Proof of Utah residency (utility bill, lease, or state-issued document)
U.S. citizenship or immigration status documentation
Most recent Social Security award letter
Pension or retirement income statements
Bank statements for all accounts (last 3 months)
Investment documents (stocks, bonds, annuities)
Life insurance policies with face values
Vehicle titles and registration
Pre-need burial contracts or irrevocable burial trusts
Medical records documenting need for daily assistance
Doctor documentation of medical conditions
List of current medications
Medicare and other health insurance cards

contacts.label: Utah Medicaid
contacts.description: Main program administrator

contacts.label: Local County Aging Services
contacts.description: Contact your county's Aging & Adult Services office for intake and assessment

applicationNotes: Income limits update annually in March (not January like federal programs) — current limit is $1,305/month through February 2026, then increases to $1,330/month in March 2026
Spousal income is exempt even if living together, but each spouse has separate asset limits if both are applying
60-month look-back rule applies — transfers of assets below fair market value in the past 5 years may result in a penalty period
This is different from New Choices Waiver (which has higher income limit of $2,901/month) — make sure you're applying for the right program

faqs.question: Can my loved one keep their house if it's worth more than $2,000?
faqs.answer: Yes, the primary home is exempt from the $2,000 asset limit as long as the home equity is under $730,000 and your loved one (or their spouse) lives there or intends to return. The home value doesn't count toward the asset limit regardless of how much it's worth.

faqs.question: What if my loved one has dementia but can still dress and bathe themselves?
faqs.answer: Dementia diagnosis alone is not sufficient to qualify.
