Your loved one must meet 2 of 3 specific conditions: need significant physical help with at least 2 daily activities, have poor orientation requiring nursing facility care, or have a medical condition that can't be safely managed without waiver services.

faqs.question: Can I apply on behalf of my elderly loved one?
faqs.answer: Yes, adult children can help with the application process and speak on behalf of their loved one. You'll need to provide all the required documentation and participate in the assessment process with your loved one.

faqs.question: How much will my loved one receive in services each month?
faqs.answer: There are no fixed dollar or hour limits. Services are tailored to your loved one's specific needs through a personalized care plan developed with a case manager. The amount depends on what services they need and what the assessment determines is necessary.

faqs.question: What's the difference between Utah Aging Waiver and New Choices Waiver?
faqs.answer: Utah Aging Waiver is for people 65+ with lower incomes ($1,305/month limit). New Choices Waiver has a higher income limit ($2,901/month) but serves a different target group. Make sure you're applying for the program that matches your loved one's age and income level.

faqs.question: What happens if my loved one's income increases after enrollment?
faqs.answer: Income limits are checked during annual renewals. If your loved one's income exceeds the limit ($1,305/month currently), they may lose eligibility for the waiver. However, small increases due to cost-of-living adjustments may be accommodated since the income limits also increase annually in March.

faqs.question: Can my loved one get personal care services right away after approval?
faqs.answer: Once approved, you'll work with a case manager to develop a care plan and identify service providers. The timeline for starting services depends on provider availability in your area and the specific services needed in your loved one's care plan.

faqs.question: What if we gave money to family members in the past 5 years?
faqs.answer: The 60-month look-back rule means any asset transfers below fair market value in the past 5 years could result in a penalty period that delays eligibility. You'll need to document all transfers and their purposes during the application process.

stateOverview.intro:
Utah offers 8 financial benefit programs and 2 free resource programs to help your aging loved one. Financial help ranges from $190-$850 toward utility bills (LIHEAP) to up to $1,500/year in caregiver support services, plus monthly grocery assistance up to $415 and comprehensive Medicaid coverage for medical and long-term care costs. Free resources include one-on-one Medicare counseling and legal help for seniors facing benefit issues or elder abuse.

Most financial programs require income verification, but free resources like Medicare counseling through Utah SHIP are available regardless of income. Start by checking if your loved one qualifies for Medicaid — it's Utah's most comprehensive program covering both medical costs and home-based care services.

startHere.name: Utah Medicaid Program
startHere.why: Covers the biggest expenses — nursing home care, home-based services, and medical costs for seniors on limited incomes.

startHere.name: Utah SNAP
startHere.why: Quick financial relief with up to $415/month for groceries loaded directly onto an EBT card.

startHere.name: Utah SHIP
startHere.why: Free Medicare counseling available to everyone — helps find cost assistance programs you might be missing.

byNeed.need: Help paying for home care and medical costs
byNeed.description: Medicaid covers comprehensive medical and long-term care, while the Aging Waiver specifically helps parents stay home instead of moving to nursing facilities.
SNAP provides monthly grocery money, while Meals on Wheels delivers prepared meals for parents who can't shop or cook.

byNeed.need: Help with utility bills and home energy costs
byNeed.description: LIHEAP provides immediate bill payment assistance, while Weatherization makes free home improvements to reduce long-term energy costs.
Up to $1,500/year in respite care, personal care aides, and other services specifically for family members providing care.
SHIP provides Medicare counseling while Legal Services offers free legal help with benefits, elder abuse, and estate planning.

quickFacts: Utah does not currently offer PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly), unlike many other states
Crisis energy assistance through LIHEAP can provide up to $2,000 for emergency heating or cooling situations
Most financial programs require income verification, but exact limits vary by program and household size
stateOverview.resourcesVsBenefits: Utah's benefit programs require qualification based on income, assets, or care needs — like Medicaid's income limits or SNAP's fixed-income requirements. But Utah's free resource programs are available to any senior regardless of financial situation: Utah SHIP provides Medicare counseling to anyone 60+, and Utah Legal Services offers free legal help to any senior facing benefit issues, housing problems, or elder abuse.
